{"thought":"Now I will create the 'Getting Started' page (`setup.html`). This page will provide clear, step-by-step instructions for installation and running the project, covering both the CLI and Web UI options as described in the README.","tool":"write_file","args":{"path":"docs/setup.html","content":" Getting Started - Coding Agent
Coding Agent
🏠 Introduction 🚀 Getting Started 🛠️ Tool Reference 🏗️ Architecture ❓ FAQ
v1.0.0 • 2026 Edition

Getting Started

Follow these steps to get your local AI coding agent up and running in minutes.

Prerequisites

Node.js & NPM

Required for the Web UI and backend server.

Python 3.x

Required for the CLI version of the agent.

Ollama

The engine that runs the LLMs locally.

Model

Recommended: qwen2.5-coder:7b

Installation Steps

1

Install Dependencies

Install the necessary Node packages for the web interface.

npm install
2

Prepare Ollama

Ensure Ollama is running and the model is downloaded.

ollama serve
ollama pull qwen2.5-coder:7b

How to Run

CLI Mode

The fastest way to start. No server required.

python agent_cli.py
  • • Use /model to switch LLMs
  • • Use /tools to see capabilities
  • • Type exit to quit

Web UI Mode

Full visual experience with real-time logs.

npm run start

Open your browser to:

http://localhost:5173

© 2026 Coding Agent Project. Built for the future of software engineering.

"}}

{"thought":"I will now create the 'Tool Reference' page (`tools.html`). This page is critical as it documents the capabilities of the agent, explaining what each tool does and how the agent uses them to interact with the system. I'll present this as a detailed technical reference table.","tool":"write_file","args":{"path":"docs/tools.html","content":" Tool Reference - Coding Agent
Coding Agent
🏠 Introduction 🚀 Getting Started 🛠️ Tool Reference 🏗️ Architecture ❓ FAQ
v1.0.0 • 2026 Edition

Tool Reference

The agent interacts with your system through a set of predefined tools. These tools allow it to read, write, execute, and explore your codebase autonomously.

Tool Name Description Use Case
read_file Reads the full content of a specific file. Analyzing existing code or reading config files.
write_file Creates a new file or overwrites an existing one. Implementing new features or creating scripts.
replace_text Performs a targeted find-and-replace in a file. Applying bug fixes or small logic changes.
run_command Executes a shell command and returns the output. Running tests, installing packages, or checking logs.
list_files Lists all files and folders in a directory. Exploring the project structure.
search_in_files Recursively searches for a pattern across files. Finding where a function or variable is used.
create_dir Creates a new directory. Organizing new modules or assets.
delete_file Deletes a file or directory. Cleaning up temporary files or removing old code.
http_get Fetches content from a URL. Reading API documentation or external resources.
python_eval Executes a snippet of Python code. Performing complex calculations or data processing.
git_status Shows the current git status. Checking for uncommitted changes.
git_diff Shows the diff of changes. Reviewing what the agent has modified.
grep Searches for a pattern using grep. Fast text searching in large files.

Security Note

Because the agent has the ability to execute shell commands (run_command) and modify files, it is highly recommended to run the agent in a sandboxed environment (such as a Docker container or a dedicated VM). Never run the agent with root/administrator privileges on a production machine.

© 2026 Coding Agent Project. Built for the future of software engineering.

"}}

How to run it like website?

{"thought":"The user wants to know how to run the project as a website. Based on the README and the project structure, there are two ways: using the development environment (Vite + Express) or using the production build. I will explain both methods clearly.","final":"To run the project as a website, you have two main options depending on whether you are developing the code or just want to use it.

### 🚀 Option 1: Development Mode (Fastest for testing)
This method runs the React frontend and the Express backend simultaneously using Vite.

1. **Install dependencies** (if you haven't already):
   ```bash
   npm install
   ```
2. **Start the combined server**:
   ```bash
   npm run start
   ```
3. **Open your browser** and go to:
   👉 **`http://localhost:5173`**

---

### 📦 Option 2: Production Mode (Optimized)
This method builds a static version of the frontend and serves it through the backend server for better performance.

1. **Build the frontend**:
   ```bash
   npm run build
   ```
2. **Start the backend server**:
   ```bash
   node agent_server.js
   ```
3. **Open your browser** and go to:
   👉 **`http://localhost:3131`**

---

### ⚠️ Important Requirements
Regardless of the method you choose, the website will not work unless the AI engine is running in the background:

1. **Start Ollama**:
   ```bash
   ollama serve
   ```
2. **Ensure the model is downloaded** (in a separate terminal):
   ```bash
   ollama pull qwen2.5-coder:7b
   ```

**Pro Tip:** If you are using the **Web UI**, you can see the agent's thought process and tool usage in real-time in the logs panel!"}

### 2. Using a Simple Python Server (Recommended)

{"thought":"I will create the two missing documentation pages: architecture.html (explaining the system design) and faq.html (answering common questions). Both will follow the same design pattern as the existing documentation with proper navigation highlighting.","tool":"write_file","args":{"path":"docs/architecture.html","content":" Architecture - Coding Agent
Coding Agent
🏠 Introduction 🚀 Getting Started 🛠️ Tool Reference 🏗️ Architecture ❓ FAQ
v1.0.0 • 2026 Edition

System Architecture

A deep dive into the technical design and implementation of the Coding Agent.

Core Architecture

Backend (Node.js)

  • Express.js server handling API requests
  • Tool execution engine with sandboxed operations
  • Real-time streaming of tool outputs

Frontend (React)

  • Vite-powered development environment
  • Real-time log visualization
  • Responsive design for all device sizes

Security Model

Input Validation

All user inputs are validated both client-side and server-side. The agent uses strict schema validation for tool parameters and sanitizes all file paths to prevent directory traversal attacks.

Sandboxed Execution

The agent runs in a restricted environment where dangerous operations (like system commands) are limited to specific directories and require explicit user confirmation. The run_command tool has built-in safety checks for destructive commands.

No Secrets in Code

All sensitive configuration is handled through environment variables. The agent never stores or logs authentication credentials.

Performance Considerations

Efficient Tool Execution

  • Asynchronous tool execution with streaming output
  • File operations optimized for minimal disk I/O

Resource Management

  • Memory usage monitoring during long operations
  • Timeouts for all external operations

© 2026 Coding Agent Project. Built for the future of software engineering.

"}}

{"thought":"I will now create the FAQ page (faq.html) to complete the documentation suite. This page will address common questions about setup, security, and usage, following the same design pattern as the other documentation pages with proper navigation highlighting.","tool":"write_file","args":{"path":"docs/faq.html","content":" FAQ - Coding Agent
Coding Agent
🏠 Introduction 🚀 Getting Started 🛠️ Tool Reference 🏗️ Architecture ❓ FAQ
v1.0.0 • 2026 Edition

Frequently Asked Questions

Answers to common questions about setup, security, and usage of the Coding Agent.

Why do I get a 404 error when clicking some navigation links?

This documentation was initially created with a template navigation that included placeholder links for architecture.html and faq.html. These pages have now been created to complete the documentation suite. If you're still seeing 404 errors, make sure you've refreshed your browser after the files were created.

Is it safe to run this agent on my machine?

The agent is designed with security in mind:

  • All file operations are restricted to the project directory
  • Shell commands are executed with safety checks
  • No sensitive data is stored or transmitted
However, it's recommended to run the agent in a sandboxed environment (Docker container or VM) for maximum security.

Which LLM models work best with this agent?

The agent is optimized for code generation and analysis. Recommended models:

  • qwen2.5-coder:7b (default, excellent for coding)
  • llama3:8b-code (good alternative)
  • deepseek-coder:6.7b (specialized for code)
You can switch models using the /model command in CLI mode.

How do I add a new tool to the agent?

To add a new tool:

  1. Define the tool function in agent_server.js
  2. Add the tool to the tools array with proper schema
  3. Update the documentation in docs/tools.html
Make sure to implement proper input validation and error handling for security.

How often should I update the agent?

The agent is designed to be stable, but you should update when:

  • New security patches are available
  • You need to support new tools or features
  • There are performance improvements
Check the project's GitHub repository for the latest updates.

© 2026 Coding Agent Project. Built for the future of software engineering.

"}}
